ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for potato and beef mixed noodles; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for potato-beef noodle dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, representing a single bowl of potato and beef mixed noodles as typically served at a Lanzhou-style noodle restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup / mixed beef noodle dish — estimated from USDA Foundation Foods component data: cooked wheat noodles (USDA FDC ID 168936), braised beef chuck (USDA FDC ID 174032), boiled potato (USDA FDC ID 170026), soy sauce (USDA FDC ID 172238), sesame oil (USDA FDC ID 172239)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Potato and Beef Mixed Noodles' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition: approximately 55% cooked wheat noodles, 25% braised beef, 15% boiled potato, and 5% sauce/condiments (soy sauce, sesame oil, chilli oil). Per-100g values are derived from this blended composition using USDA Foundation Foods and SR Legacy data. Values represent a plausible central estimate for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed noodle dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Wheat noodles, boiled' (GI 52, glucose reference) and 'Beef noodle soup' category data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Potato and Beef Mixed Noodles'. GI estimated at 52 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, low category). The potato component (boiled potato GI ~50–56) and beef (negligible GI contribution) are consistent with this estimate. The mixed dish GI is anchored to the dominant carbohydrate source (wheat noodles ~55% by weight). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 87.75) / 100 ≈ 45.6, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 19.5 × 4.5 = 87.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium seasoning, as a single 450g serving provides approximately 1,710 mg sodium, representing 74–114% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1,500–2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and high total carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 450g serving delivers approximately 88g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~46, which is high; portion reduction to half a bowl and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from soy-based seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of this dish due to its saturated fat content (~6.3g per serving) and high sodium load (~1,710 mg per serving); choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting lighter seasoning can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato and Beef Mixed Noodles (土豆牛肉拌面, Tǔdòu Niúròu Bàn Miàn) is a Chinese noodle dish originating from the Lanzhou culinary tradition of Gansu Province, China, and served at Lanzhou-style beef noodle restaurants in Singapore. The dish consists of hand-pulled or machine-cut wheat noodles tossed or mixed with braised or stewed beef, soft-cooked potato chunks, and a savoury sauce typically composed of soy sauce, chilli oil, sesame paste or sesame oil, garlic, and ginger. Unlike Lanzhou beef noodle soup (which is broth-based), this is a dry-mixed or semi-dry preparation where the noodles are coated in the sauce rather than submerged in broth. The texture profile features chewy wheat noodles, tender braised beef, and soft potato, with a bold umami-forward, mildly spicy, and nutty flavour from the sesame and chilli components. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy mixed meal providing approximately 148 kcal per 100g, with meaningful protein from beef (~8.5g/100g), moderate carbohydrate from noodles and potato (~19.5g/100g), and relatively low fat (~4.2g/100g). The primary clinical concern is sodium content, which is elevated due to soy sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category), anchored to the wheat noodle component, though the glycemic load per standard serving is high due to the large carbohydrate volume. This dish is suitable for general consumption but requires portion awareness for individuals with diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular conditions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted-ingredient composition model using USDA Foundation Foods and SR Legacy component data. The GI is an analogous estimate anchored to boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-characterised and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition (approximately 55% cooked wheat noodles, 25% braised beef, 15% boiled potato, 5% sauce/condiments) using USDA Foundation Foods and SR Legacy data; no exact dish-level entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. The dish is served at Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles (兰亭序) in Singapore; halal status is not confirmed and is marked false pending restaurant-level verification. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 87.75) / 100 ≈ 45.6, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 87.75g. 6. Sodium estimate of 380 mg/100g reflects typical soy-sauce-seasoned Chinese noodle dishes; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.1 reflects trace amounts from beef; this is naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) rather than industrial trans fat.
